messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant which rephrase the following text in your own words.
I would be happy to help! Please provide me with the text you would like me to rephrase.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Most podcast apps will search episode titles. So your episodes become a portfolio that helps your podcast be found for all kinds of keywords! Instead of trying to stuff those SEO terms into your podcast title (and risk getting kicked out of podcast apps), make separate, well-titled episodes about those subjects you want people to search for and find your podcast. This helps in podcast apps, websites, and social networks.

The majority of podcast applications have the capability to search for specific episode titles. This means that your episodes can act as a sort of portfolio, increasing your chances of being discovered through various keywords. Instead of attempting to cram SEO terms into your podcast title (which could potentially result in being removed from podcast apps), it is advisable to create individual episodes with well-crafted titles that align with the subjects you want people to search for. By doing so, you enhance your podcast's discoverability on podcast apps, websites, and social networks.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: If your old episodes have good search-engine optimization (SEO), then I highly recommend keeping them available, both on your website and in your podcast feed. This will ensure your podcast can still be found for those things you talked about and found wherever people are looking.

I strongly suggest retaining your old episodes, especially if they have been optimized for search engines (SEO). By making them accessible on your website and in your podcast feed, you can ensure that your podcast remains discoverable for the various topics you discussed. This way, your content can continue to be found wherever people are searching for it.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: This approach works great for podcasts that aren't designed to sell something (like your own products and services) except for their own inherent value. And this only works when the audience will actually want those old episodes. I see this used most often for entertaining podcasts, like a comedy show where your old episodes are just as funny as the new episodes, or the older seasons of a drama are available only for sale while you keep your current season free.

This strategy is highly effective for podcasts that are not primarily focused on selling products or services, but instead aim to provide value in and of themselves. However, it is essential to consider whether the audience would have a genuine interest in accessing those older episodes. This approach is often employed by entertaining podcasts, such as comedy shows where the humor in previous episodes remains just as enjoyable as the new ones. Similarly, in the case of a drama series, older seasons may be available for purchase while the current season is offered for free.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: If your episodes regularly help build your PROFIT, then it would probably be most profitable for you to keep them online as long as possible. If they have good SEO, they'll continue attracting people. And if they have good calls to action or other PROFIT-building value, then they'll probably continue returning that value for as long as they stay online!

If your episodes consistently contribute to your profitability, it would be advantageous to keep them available online for as long as possible. By maintaining good search engine optimization (SEO), they will continue to attract an audience. Additionally, if these episodes include strong calls to action or provide other forms of value that contribute to your profitability, they are likely to continue delivering value as long as they remain accessible online.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For example, I still get a request every now and then for me to design a website or podcast cover art for someone because I had promoted those personalized services back when I offered them myself—years ago! (I now refer that out, so you're still welcome to ask for my recommended referrals!) And I continue to receive new subscribers to my email newsletter through past episodes that promoted exclusive resources. I also continue to receive new members to My Podcast Reviews and customers to my other products and services because of how they were relevantly promoted in timeless content, even from years ago!

To illustrate this, on occasion, I receive requests to design websites or create podcast cover art for individuals. This is a result of promoting these personalized services myself, even though it was years ago (I now refer such requests to others, but I'm still available to offer recommended referrals if needed!). Moreover, I continue to gain new subscribers for my email newsletter through past episodes that promoted exclusive resources. By leveraging timeless content, I consistently attract new members to My Podcast Reviews and acquire customers for my various other products and services.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: A complication arises when you have so many episodes and such large notes for each episode that your podcast RSS feed grows to multiple megabytes (MB). With my own article-style notes for each episode, multiplied by the hundreds of episodes I've published, I only recently realized my own RSS feed was over 5 MB! I never really noticed because I use Blubrry's Podcast Mirror service as a proxy for my RSS feed, to keep it fast and stable. But it was still so big that some podcast apps and services were starting to choke on it, even causing some old episodes to not display anymore.

A challenge arises when the number of episodes and extensive notes for each episode leads to a significant increase in the size of your podcast's RSS feed, resulting in multiple megabytes (MB) of data. I personally encountered this issue with my own extensive article-style notes for each episode, and over time, I discovered that my RSS feed had exceeded 5 MB. This went largely unnoticed due to my use of Blubrry's Podcast Mirror service, which acts as a proxy to keep my RSS feed fast and stable. However, the large size of the feed began causing problems, with certain podcast apps and services struggling to handle it effectively, resulting in the failure to display some older episodes.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I could have reduced the number of episodes in my feed, but then that would have cost me SEO (#3) and PROFIT (#5). And until my proposal to support notes in chapters is adopted into Podcasting 2.0, or PowerPress changes how it generates podcast feeds from WordPress, I didn't want to remove my full articles from my RSS feed. Instead, I enabled PowerPress's “Feed Episode Maximizer,” which allows full content on only my latest 10 episodes, and then minimal content (especially omitting my lengthy articles) on all older episodes. This reduced my feed with nearly 400 episodes from over 5 MB to the sweet spot of under 1 MB.

If I had chosen to decrease the number of episodes in my RSS feed, it would have had negative implications for both SEO and profitability. Additionally, I was hesitant to remove the full articles from my RSS feed until my proposal for supporting notes in chapters was implemented in Podcasting 2.0 or PowerPress changed their approach to generating podcast feeds from WordPress. Instead, I opted to activate PowerPress's "Feed Episode Maximizer" feature. This allowed me to display the full content of only the latest 10 episodes, while minimizing the content, particularly excluding lengthy articles, on older episodes. As a result, the size of my feed, which originally exceeded 5 MB with nearly 400 episodes, was significantly reduced to a more optimal size of under 1 MB.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Most podcast apps track each podcast episode by the episode's globally unique identifier (GUID). This is an RSS tag you don't have to worry about unless you're migrating hosting providers or publishing tools—in which case, worry a lot because the GUIDs should never change! Some apps will remember only the GUIDs that still exist in the feed and forget anything no longer in the feed. When you expand the feed to include more old episodes, those old episodes might look completely new to podcast apps because the apps have forgotten the GUIDs or they've never seen those episode GUIDs). Then, the app will probably download or redownload those old episodes, even if they were already played. At the least, they might be displayed as new and unplayed, and thus confuse or frustrate your audience.

In most podcast apps, each episode is identified using a globally unique identifier (GUID). Unless you are switching hosting providers or publishing tools, you generally don't have to worry about the GUID. However, if you do find yourself in a situation where a migration is necessary, it is crucial to pay close attention to the GUIDs, as they should never be modified. Some apps have the capability to remember only the GUIDs that remain in the podcast feed, disregarding anything that is no longer present. Therefore, when you expand the feed to include older episodes, these episodes may appear brand new to the podcast apps, since the apps have either forgotten the GUIDs or have never encountered them before. As a result, the app may proceed to download or redownload those old episodes, even if they had already been played by the listener. At the very least, they might be displayed as new and unplayed, potentially causing confusion or frustration for your audience.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Alternatively, you could create a separate RSS feed to hold your older episodes, like 100 episodes at a time, each with its full-text content. This would keep those old episodes available (especially if you submit the archive feeds to podcast apps and directories), but their own SEO would build the archive feed's ranking, not that of your active feed. If you make archive feeds, this would be a great opportunity to do two important things:

An alternative approach would be to generate a distinct RSS feed dedicated to housing your older episodes. This can be done by grouping them into sets of 100 episodes, with each episode containing its complete textual content. By implementing this method, your older episodes can remain accessible, particularly if you submit the archive feeds to various podcast apps and directories. However, it is important to note that the SEO efforts applied to these archive feeds would contribute towards boosting the ranking of the archive feed itself, rather than impacting the ranking of your active feed. Creating archive feeds presents an excellent opportunity to accomplish two essential objectives:

Rephrase the following text in your own words: When old episodes reappear in your RSS feed, you might also have similar troubles with some automation tools. For example, something that automatically tweets whenever you publish a new episode. But from my observations, most of these tools look for only what's new at the top of an RSS feed (where new items are) or only episodes the have a date more recent than the last time the automation ran. Thus, reappearing old episodes probably won't trigger any automations, but it's still possible, and you might not even know until it happens.

If older episodes resurface in your RSS feed, it is possible that you may encounter issues with certain automation tools as well. For instance, if you have a tool that automatically tweets whenever a new episode is published. However, based on my observations, most of these tools typically search for new content at the top of an RSS feed, where the latest items are located, or specifically target episodes with a date more recent than the last automation run. As a result, the reappearance of old episodes is unlikely to activate any automations. However, there is still a chance that it could occur, and you may only become aware of it when it actually happens.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Podcasting 2.0 introduced a new way for your audience to support your podcast by sending micropayments. This is usually measured by satoshis (abbreviated as “sats”), which are one hundred millionths of a Bitcoin. As such a small portion of cryptocurrency, many people might wonder whether the effort is even worth the return.

The advent of Podcasting 2.0 has ushered in a novel method for your audience to show support for your podcast through micropayments. These payments are typically measured using satoshis, which are minute fractions of a Bitcoin, equivalent to one hundred millionth of the currency. Due to the minute nature of this cryptocurrency unit, many individuals may question whether the effort involved in making such small payments is truly worthwhile in terms of the corresponding return.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: As you can probably guess from the word itself, micropayments are very small transactions. The business world often considers any transaction below $20 to be a micropayment. But where our personal finances are concerned, we might think of micropayments closer to $5 or below.

As the term implies, micropayments refer to transactions of a very small monetary value. In the corporate realm, any transaction below $20 is typically deemed a micropayment. However, when it comes to our personal finances, we tend to perceive micropayments as being closer to $5 or even lower in value.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: With a Podcasting 2.0 podcast app, audiences can stream satoshis (“sats”) to Podcasting 2.0 compatible podcasts by automatically sending small amounts (such as 100 or 1,000 sats) for every minute of listening. Another way is to send a “boostagram,” which is a one-time payment that can include a message—the “gram” part comes from the idea of a telegram, and the “boost” part (a term often used in podcast apps) comes from the idea of boosting how much you're supporting because you liked something you heard at a particular point in the episode. Perhaps even boosting beyond the amount you normally stream to the podcast.

Using a Podcasting 2.0 podcast app, listeners have the ability to stream satoshis, referred to as "sats," to Podcasting 2.0 compatible podcasts. This process involves automatically sending small amounts, like 100 or 1,000 sats, for each minute of listening time. Alternatively, listeners can also opt to send a "boostagram," which is a one-time payment that includes a message. The term "boostagram" combines the concept of a telegram (traditionally used for communication) with the notion of "boosting" support for a podcast by contributing more when the listener particularly enjoys something heard at a specific point in the episode. This "boost" can exceed the standard streaming amount typically contributed to the podcast.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You might hear some podcasters mention their boosts or streams from only a couple hundred sats to a hundred thousand or more. While these numbers seem large, they still convert to small amounts in USD (at least as of March 2023).

It is not uncommon to come across podcasters discussing their boosts or streams ranging from a few hundred sats to even tens of thousands or more. Although these numbers may appear significant, it is important to note that when converted to USD, they still amount to relatively small sums (at least as of March 2023).

Rephrase the following text in your own words: It used to be that the only way you could take credit card payments was with a “merchant account” that would have its own monthly fees. But PayPal, Stripe, and other services have made it far more affordable and accessible to receive payments, including ACH transfers, paying from a balance, or debit/credit-card payments.

In the past, accepting credit card payments typically required a "merchant account" that incurred monthly fees. However, services like PayPal, Stripe, and others have revolutionized the payment landscape, making it more affordable and accessible to receive payments. These services offer various options for transactions, such as ACH transfers, payments from existing balances, as well as debit and credit card payments.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You might already see the problem: if your audience wanted to donate $1 via PayPal, you would get less than half of that after PayPal's 2.89% fee and the 49¢ flat fee. Even worse, those fees will likely be even higher outside the USA!

The issue becomes apparent when considering that if your audience intends to donate $1 via PayPal, you would receive less than half of that amount after deducting PayPal's fees of 2.89% and the additional 49¢ flat fee. Furthermore, these fees are likely to be even higher for transactions made outside of the United States, exacerbating the problem.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Losing around 50¢ might not seem like much, but multiply that by how many people might be willing to give you $1 donations per episode or per month. At that small of an individual donation, you would lose half your income in payment fees!

While losing approximately 50¢ per transaction may not appear significant, when multiplied by the number of individuals who are willing to contribute $1 donations per episode or per month, the impact becomes substantial. With such small individual donations, payment fees could potentially account for half of your income, resulting in a significant loss.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: The “value” feature in the Podcasting 2.0 standard (added with the <podcast:value> RSS tag) allows you to receive sats from your audience via a Podcasting 2.0 podcast app. They can choose to send any amount of satoshis, and you will probably pay only a small percentage in fees—but no additional flat fees!

The Podcasting 2.0 standard introduces the "value" feature, which is incorporated using the <podcast:value> RSS tag. This feature enables you to receive satoshis from your audience through a Podcasting 2.0 podcast app. Listeners have the freedom to send any desired amount of satoshis. The noteworthy advantage is that you will likely incur only a small percentage of fees, without any additional flat fees.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Consider the $1 example again. Legacy payment systems would take around half of that. But receiving the equivalent amount in Satoshis would cost you only around 3–5¢, depending on the small fees in the payment chain. While that 3–5% could obviously be higher than other payment methods, that percentage is all you pay!

Let's revisit the $1 example once more. Traditional payment systems would deduct approximately half of that amount. However, if you were to receive the equivalent value in Satoshis, the associated costs would be as low as 3-5¢, taking into account the minimal fees within the payment network. While this 3-5% might be slightly higher than other payment methods, it is the sole percentage that you would need to pay, avoiding any additional charges.
